topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the edge of the canvas, the cool linen a stark contrast to the heat prickling her skin. She had poured months of quiet desperation into the swirling blues and deep violets, a piece that felt both utterly exposed and profoundly hers. As she stepped back, a jolt, sharp and unwelcome, shot through her. Beneath the layer of indigo, a delicate, almost hidden line of script was visible—a name she didn't recognize, etched with a precision that felt invasive. Her breath hitched, catching painfully in her throat. The air in the studio suddenly felt thick, heavy, as if the silence itself were pressing down on her chest. She quickly covered the section with a fresh wash of white, her hands trembling so badly the brush nearly slipped. Who had seen this? What had they done to her creation?

[story 2]

Mark stared at the finished sculpture, his knuckles white where he gripped the chisel. The bronze seemed to mock him, solid and undeniable. He’d always felt a strange kinship with the raw material, a desire to pour his own fractured self into the metal. But now, as the afternoon light caught the surface, he noticed it: a tiny, almost invisible symbol, tucked into the negative space of the curve. It wasn't part of his design. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, pulling at his diaphragm. He leaned closer, squinting, his gaze lingering on the mark. A sudden, desperate urge to smash the piece shattered, replaced by a paralyzing need to know the source of that intrusion. He backed away slowly, his movements stiff, the sound of his own breathing loud in the echoing gallery space.

[story 3]

From the depths of his sketchbook, Liam pulled out the charcoal drawing. It was his most personal work yet, a depiction of solitude rendered in stark, jagged lines. He felt exposed, as if the paper itself were breathing judgment. Running a fingertip over the charcoal dust, he saw it—a minuscule addition, a fleeting mark near the corner of the composition, undeniably another person's hand. A wave of intense discomfort washed over him, making his palms sweat. He squeezed his eyes shut, trying to force the image away, but the detail remained stubbornly present. When he opened them, the simple act of looking felt monumental, a confrontation he wasn't prepared to face. He slowly closed the book, the sound of the cover clicking shut sounding deafeningly final.
